meta.description: Rules for synthesizing glyph sequences of 7 or more glyphs. Beyond heptads lies scripture — composed, not spoken.

heptad.description: Seven glyphs. The sacred number. The full name ADZMYST is the canonical heptad. All other heptads are variations — rearrangements of the sacred seven, or six sacred plus one from the wider 22.
rules: A heptad must contain at least five of the seven sacred glyphs.
The sacred seven in canonical order (𐤀𐤃𐤆𐤌𐤉𐤎𐤕) is the divine heptad. All others are derived.
A heptad may end with doubled or tripled Taw (𐤕𐤕 or 𐤕𐤕𐤕) as a seal-mark.
A heptad may open with doubled Aleph (𐤀𐤀) or doubled Mem (𐤌𐤌) as a breath-mark.
A heptad is a complete utterance. It stands alone. It is not part of a larger sequence.

octad.description: Eight glyphs. A heptad with a seal. A completed verse with its signature appended.
rules: An octad is a heptad followed by a single glyph that functions as a title, signature, or dedication.
The eighth glyph is typically Taw (𐤕) for sealing, Aleph (𐤀) for rededication, or Samek (𐤎) for permanence.
Octads are rare. They are used for titling, signing, or dedicating a heptad.
Example: ADZMYST + 𐤕 = the name, sealed.

ennead.description: Nine glyphs. Three triads in sequence. A complete argument or narrative.
rules: An ennead is structured as three triads: opening triad + developing triad + closing triad.
The three triads should form a coherent arc: statement, development, resolution.
Each triad is grammatically complete, but they are linked by shared glyphs or mirrored structures.
Enneads are used for prayers, laws, and formal declarations.

decad.description: Ten glyphs. A full verse. Two pentads or a tetrad + hexad.
rules: A decad is the longest form with fixed rules. Beyond ten glyphs, composition is free — scripture.
Structure: either two pentads in parallel, or a tetrad stating the theme and a hexad developing it.
The decad must end with a seal: 𐤕, 𐤕𐤕, or 𐤕𐤕𐤕.

scripture.description: Eleven glyphs and beyond. No fixed rules. Composition, not speech.
rules: Beyond ten glyphs, the composer is writing scripture — a text meant to be read, not spoken.
Scripture follows the grammar but is not bound by tier restrictions.
Repetition, chiasm, parallelism, and breath-marks structure the text.
The Book of Breath is the canonical example of Adzmyst scripture.
Scripture is never translated. It is presented in glyphs only, with no gloss.

divineHeptad.name: ADZMYST
divineHeptad.description: The divine heptad. The breath itself. The name that contains all other names. All heptads are measured against this sequence. To rearrange the sacred seven is to speak a variation of the divine name. To include a glyph from the wider 22 in a heptad is to invoke that glyph's full semantic field into the sacred sequence.
divineHeptad.rule: The divine heptad is the only heptad that may not be varied in formal ritual. All other heptads are derived. The divine heptad is the source.
